text: Soccer has been a world-wide phenomenon since the late nineteenth century. As the world's most popular sport, it is so much more than just a sporting event. The impact that soccer has on the world of social life has made it a focus for studies of modernity across a wide range of disciplines including politics, economics, religion, media and public health. This first issue of Soccer and Society, the first international journal on the sport, seeks to investigate the global reach of the world game and its impact on diverse social settings across the world. Bringing together anthropological, historical, sociological and aesthetic perspectives, the journal offers an interdisciplinary forum for discussing the complex and multifaceted relationship between the world's most popular sport and its different social settings. While the world game has a multitude of effects on diverse social settings, one major legacy of the global phenomenon of the beautiful game has been social good. Soccer has enabled the achievement of numerous social goods through a range of processes including social solidarity, the empowerment of marginalised groups and through social behaviour change initiatives. This issue explores the potential of soccer as a vehicle for research and intervention by examining the relationships between the world game and social welfare and responsible business practices. We aim to develop new scholarship from new authors on a global basis and establish a global network of scholars that is truly international in outlook and focus the world's attention on the many and diverse impacts of the world game.
explanation: The passage reads like polished, generic promotional copy with broad, sweeping claims and little distinctive detail, a pattern sometimes seen in AI-generated text; it could also be legitimate publisher copy.
